speaker: interviewer
text: We can begin to wrap it up if that's OK with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever you say. Whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, because now the war is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm just going to ask you a couple more questions. And we'll do the photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the kids will be back, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? Because I think-- is there anything-- I'll wait until the tape rolls. OK. There's a couple things I want to ask you. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I try to let you tell your whole story. And then there are things if I feel that need to be covered.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure this-- listen, how can I tell my story in--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what everyone says. Everyone wants 10 days to tell their story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't do it in 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: It'd take a year, right? You ready, Crane.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most people, most survivors will die with their secrets deep inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Cleveland, April 16. This is tape five. We're with Betty Gold. You're all set again, Betty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're in the DP camp in Bindermichel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long, how long were you in Bindermichel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about, I don't know, maybe eight months or nine months, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were there with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, my brother. And the entire camp was one big happy family. And everyone-- I guess we had nothing to worry about. Nobody worked. There was nothing to do. We were refugees waiting to either go to Israel or go to the United States, whichever came first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody was waiting to leave, so what do you do in the meantime? You live on charity. And you, and you sit around and tell stories. There's nothing really-- and the young people socialized. As a matter of fact, that's where I met my first boyfriend, in the DP camps. We had a wonderful time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're just waiting. And it wasn't good. We didn't have clothes. We didn't have any-- just minimum necessities, of course. I mean, I wore a pair of stockings to a dance. And they were torn to shreds. So I put on two pair so it'll cover the, the runners, so certainly didn't live in luxury. I took a German uniform. And I found a German lady who made me a dress out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's not like we really lived well. We didn't have anything. And just-- and as far as food was concerned, whatever the Joint Distribution gave us, and then, you know, that's all. And eventually, little by little, people were leaving to Israel, and some were leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my father remembered part of my aunt's and uncle's address in the United States. And he sent a telegram. And he put on the telephone number, and the real estate company, and everything. He didn't know what it meant, but he somehow remembered the spellings. And believe it or not, they, they received it. And that's when they found that we were alive. Well, eventually, my family here in the United States sent us an affidavit. And, and we arrived in the United States, joined our-- my aunts, and my uncles, and my cousins. We were a big family-- 110 people. Unfortunately, most of them-- so many of them died already.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in into the United States August 31, 1946. I arrived in New York. And they kept us at Ellis Island for two weeks, because my mother had a spot on her lungs from tuberculosis when she was 17 years old. And they wanted to send us all back. And my father, and my brother, and I said, no. If you're going to send her back, we're going, too. So they detained us for weeks there. And I don't know how it happened, but eventually, my family came to New York. And they got us out. I don't remember what they had to do to get, to get, get us out of there. But we got out. And we've been in Cleveland ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me, did you get married? Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. As the years-- I went to Russian school when I was in Russia. I went to, to, to-- in 1939 when the Russians came, I went to school in Trochenbrod. And prior there, I went to Polish school. But I continued in bits and pieces. And when I came to the United States, I was the oldest girl-- in junior high school, they put me in. And I was very unhappy and didn't like it at all. But I knew I had to learn the language. But I was very, very sad. I was-- as bad as it was. I was missing the DP camps. Because we were a family again. And then they uprooted me. The adults understood it. For me, it was very lonely and very difficult to adjust. I would go to the movies. I loved the movies. And I would go to the movies all the time. Whenever I had a little money, I would go to the movies, and go on the balconies, I remember, and sit so nobody could see me. And I would cry, and cry, and cry, and cry. I was so alone in here. And I was so unhappy. I don't know what I was missing, but I was so sorry we came here-- like I had a choice. And eventually, I did get upset with my parents. And, and I was angry with them, why they didn't go to Israel. And I felt that the Jewish people belonged, that we were traitors for coming to the United States, as opposed to going to Israel. And that's where everybody really belongs.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very angry with my parents, very angry. Why did they come here instead of going to Israel? Don't you feel guilty? I mean, I made them feel miserable. And it's just, it's just embarrassing to be alive, you know, to survive this and not go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, well, we were waiting, whichever came first. You know, we didn't want to sit in the camps for the rest of our lives, for years, to wait to back to start a life again. So this happened first. So we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you graduated from high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, yeah, I was put in a special, like an [INAUDIBLE], at John Hay High School for six months and studied English and a lot of subjects. And then they transferred me to Heights High School. I was ready to join my peers, who were much older than-- younger than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I strictly went to high school for education. I hated every moment of it. But I did rather well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I also afterwards, I, I got married right out of high school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morton, Morton Gold. He was an American-born man. And he was very nice to me. He was really the first friend that I met here, the first boy. He treated me great. And we had a nice time. And I come from a shtetl where if you have a boyfriend and you go with him for a long time, it's a commitment. You have to marry him. [LAUGHS] I mean, you just didn't date lots of boys. If you went with one man, that's it. So eventually, you know, we grew to love each other. And we got married in 1948, November 14. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three sons-- Michael, Sheldon, and Alan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you name your boys for anyone in your family who died?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my youngest son was born, Michael, I didn't want to name him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your youngest son? Isn't Michael your oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest. Didn't I say? I'm sorry. I didn't want to name him after my brother, of whom I think day and night, day and night. I, I can't stop thinking about my brother. I have visions like he's going to walk in the house all the time, that I'm going to bump into him on the street. I can't forget him. Of all the people in the world that, that, that perished, he's the one I think of the most. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was asking you, did you name any children-- I just need to know if you named anyone for anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, so when I had my first child, I really wanted to name it after my brother. But I was in denial. I just couldn't, I just couldn't do it. And I was afraid that he'll die young, too, if I name him after my brother. And I was-- I'm not a superstitious person. But I just couldn't do it. I was afraid. And I didn't. I named him after an uncle, an American uncle. When my second child was born, Sheldon, I talked to my husband. And I said, you know, I really don't know if we'll have any more kids. We didn't think we might have more kids. And I said, if I don't give my brother a name, he'll never have a name. And I said, I just have to face reality and, and do it. And I named him after my brother. And once I did it, I felt good about it. But it was a difficult thing for me to do, very difficult. Yet I didn't want him to be without a name. That's when I think was the first time that I really realized that he's dead, after I gave him a name. But still, I, I don't know why, of all the people, I see him day and night. I sometimes lie in bed. And I imagine the door is opening up. And he's walking up. And I start screaming. And sometimes I'll sit in a movie. And I'll, and I'll imagine that he's-- all of a sudden, I turn around. He's sitting next to me. Or if I'm in business and talking to people and the door opens up, oh, what if my brother. I don't know why. I just don't know why. I have grandmothers, aunts, uncles, cousins. But he, I can't let go of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your third child? Have you named him for anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: My third child was also named after uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Shelley has a name also, my grandmother. And Michael has the name of one of my grandmothers, and so forth. And my brother had children. So he named their kids after-- also after, you know, victims.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been a working woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I-- After my children were born, I was home till my youngest was about four years old. And then I started to work part-time. I was teaching nursery school for many years, part-time. And it was nice because it got me out of the house. It gave me some money. And my kids went to nursery school free. And I did that for about, I don't know, eight or nine years. And when my kids were old enough, I started to work at a retail shop. It was called [INAUDIBLE]. It was a bridal shop, and gifts, and furniture, and decorating. And I learned a lot from our decorator there. And I liked the business. I worked [? full-time. ?] And I always had lunch with my kids and dropped them off at school on the way. And then I went to work. So I worked from, like, 1:00 till 6:00. And after that, I bought in as a partner in my cousin's business, which was a lingerie shop. And from one shop, we opened up two more. So we owned three shops. And I was a very busy woman with a family, and a business to run, and shopping trips to New York, and whatnot. And I just loved it-- I just loved it to death. I just felt so productive, and useful, and exciting. I also went to school at the same time. I even went with my husband at night while my children were little, too. So I got, like, an early education degree. You only needed a two year certificate at that time. So I felt very proud of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very fortunate. I had a husband who supported me in every way possible. No matter what I wanted to do, he gave me so much confidence. He always says, you can do it. Oh, come on, you can do it. After what you went through? Come on, you know you can do it. Come on, get going. And he would help me and support me, push me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It got to the point-- and then I was in the business for about 10 years. And my cousin, who was my partner, had cancer. And I decided I couldn't run the business alone financially, and time-wise, and so forth. It was too much of a responsibility for one person with three shops. So we, we liquidated two shops and sold one. And from there, I went to work for a textile company, doing sales to hotels, decorating hotels, like draperies, and bedspreads, and carpeting, so forth, and selling the linens at the same time to them-- like pillows, and sheets, and towels. And, and I sold to nursing homes. And I sold to hospitals. And I worked until a year ago. And because I had a problem with a shoulder and my back, I was-- I decided to retire. But I don't like it one bit. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I hope to go back part-time one of these days. I think we're just about finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we're going to do next, depending on-- I have a couple more questions which I will ask you when you have your family around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want to-- do you want to film the photographs first and then the children? Is that OK with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, we can do that, either way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, yeah, I would like to do it. So then we can end with the family. And that will be nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pause tape? Yeah, so wait, one second, wait, wait, wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: To talk about this. Very little did I tell my children about my life-- just incidents, a few here and there. And I never went to the movies. I never read anything about the Holocaust, very little until recently. I, I was in denial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my theory was this. My philosophy was this-- who in the heck is going to tell me what took place? What do they know? What can they write in a 400 page book, that I lived through? And for that reason alone-- and why talk about it? Where am I going to begin? And where am I going to end? Even what I told you today, it's maybe one thousandth percent of what I went through. And for that reason, I could never imagine that anybody could write a book that is factual and all the details, that anybody who wasn't there, that could, that could see the misery, and the blood, and the killings, and the torture. So I didn't read about these. Because how can people write a book, not being there? Or even if you were there, it's impossible to describe your feelings and emotions of so many years to put down on paper, unless you wrote a daily diary. And that's the reason-- if I can't tell the whole story, why bother? It doesn't mean-- it's like a drop in the bucket.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never read literature. I never went to see the movies. I never went to-- I mean, I didn't take any part in any Holocaust celebrations or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Remembrances.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remembrances. I, I just distanced myself from it. Because I felt it's just so little when there's so much to tell. And why did it happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did-- I'll tell you what I'm angry. It's like Bosnia all over again, you know, another Holocaust. What's going on here? I mean, people bring a child from Lebanon, or from Russia, or from anywhere in the world to give them care, medical care. Maybe their brain is hurt. Maybe they have a, an eye problem or something. And they spend millions and millions of dollars to save one single person. And everybody's so interested, which is wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was killing, and torturing, and hanging, and maiming six million people. And the world stood still. Now one person means so much today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time-- and they're doing the same thing now. I mean, we have atrocities all over the world right now. And we're still standing still and being quiet. Why? Why is the world quiet? Why, why, why can't something be done to stop these killings? Why is it that the more sophisticated we get, the, the more all this communication, and all this sophistication, and all this equipment and whatnot, why don't we learn to live in peace and stop killing each other? What have we advanced? What have we accomplished? I think we're uncivilized-- more uncivilized than primitive time. I don't think this is the end of it. And I'm angry. I'm angry why the world stood still then. And I'm angry why we're standing still now and allowing all these atrocities all over the world, and starvation, and hunger. Why? Why can't the world find an answer? Why don't human beings act like human beings? I don't care if you're Jewish, or Gentile, or German, or Russian, or-- we're people. Why don't people love each other? Why don't people care about each other? You know, we feel sorry for them with every bit of news we watch, with every piece of bite of steak we take. Oh, my goodness. Did you hear about that tragedy there and there? You know what? I'm among them, too. I feel, you know, what can one person do? It's not fair. It's not fair.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're very primitive. We're very uncivilized. And I'm angry. I really am. I don't think that was the end of the Holocaust, I think it's just going to continue as long as man is alive. And I feel sorry for my grandchildren and great-grandchildren. And I just, just feel we'll never learn. I hope that somebody learns something. And I think you're making a great contribution. And if-- if it'll help having a bit of peace in this world, so be it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I was going to wait-- OK. I was going to wait to ask you about this when you were with the kids, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just like acting. I can't explain it. I'm just temporarily here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're all temporarily here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't have a right to this furniture. And I don't have a right to the food. And I don't have a right to all the luxuries, and my vacations, and my good life. I don't have a right to any of it. Now that my life is better financially and I have the, you know, whereabouts to go and do, and I feel guilty about it. I think my life hasn't changed that much at all. Financially, I mean, I just, I feel guilty for living. And when I think of my girlfriends and my cousins. One of my cousins came to the wall. And we didn't let her in, because we saw a German on the other side. And she turned around. And she got killed. And I saw that with my own eyes. She's on the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can I go on living and feel good about it? I live, and I do whatever I have to, or whatever I want to do. But I don't feel good about it. I'm not happy. It's like my whole life is a stage, or acting. Or my children, I do it for my family. It's something-- it, it tears you apart. [INAUDIBLE] [CRYING] You can't. How can I explain this to my kids? I don't want them to be upset either.

speaker: interviewee
text: I look at my grandchildren, and I say, god, I wonder if they'll be blessed with peace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's just-- but I'm older already. And I suppose I'll, I'll continue and do the best I can with what I have. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel sorry for, I feel sorry for people who suffer. I do. It's, it's a disease you can't get rid of. You have to live with it for the rest of your life. And it's not something you're anxious to pass on to your kids. You want to tell them good, happy things. I tell my grandchildren stories that my grandmother told me. Although I, I ask-- I answer their questions. And I do tell them, I do tell them little things. I don't want to upset the little ones. It's impossible. I have not told you the story, my dear. I just briefly mentioned a few happenings. The rest is deep--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, is there something else--

speaker: interviewee
text: In my heart. And some things, I suppose, subconsciously I can't even dig out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lost a great possession. I had one spoon from home. And a few years ago, I lost it. And I'm very upset. Otherwise, it's like a whole new beginning. And I don't know why I still refer to home as Trochenbrod. When I say when I was-- used to-- at home, we did it this way, or at home this, or home that, or home that, to me, home is still Trochenbrod. I don't know why. Of course, this is my home. I've lived in Cleveland all these years, since 1946. But home is still Trochenbrod to me. That's where all my people are-- dead. But that's, that's where it's all. But-- and I don't want to go there. There's nothing to see, nobody to see-- I don't want to go to Poland. And I've traveled all over the world. I've been on every continent, and many, many, many countries. You know. I don't want to go to Poland. I don't want to contribute to their well-being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what? I don't even have any animosity towards people. I'm just angry at the world. I'm just angry. I don't hate anybody. I don't even hate the German people now. I don't hate the Polish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians, I think, I'm more bitter at than anybody else. And I'll tell you, I [INAUDIBLE] them. [INAUDIBLE] if I could go back to the woods. We were a whole bunch of people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were a whole bunch of people who found a couple [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what a [INAUDIBLE] is? The [INAUDIBLE] were the Ukrainians who participated in finding Jews in the woods and, and killing them. And our group of cave people found two [INAUDIBLE]. And I'll tell you what they did with them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a tub. I don't know where they got-- a bath-- a tub with legs. They boiled water in the tub. And they put them in the tub. And they boiled them. And they made us watch. The Russians did that. The Russians did that, the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they made us watch. And I couldn't watch. I was crying. I couldn't stand it. And one of the partisans came around-- this is after what they did to your brother, after what they did to your uncles and cousins and everybody, you can't look? And they turned me around physically that I should stare.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't handle it. I couldn't handle it. I mean, I hated them. I hate them. I hate them worse than the Nazis, those [INAUDIBLE]. But I, I couldn't watch it. I couldn't take it. And some of the men and women were taking sticks and hitting them while they were boiling them, they'd say, this is for my mother, and this is for my father, and this is for my cousin, and this is for my-- they were so outraged, they were crazy. They really didn't know what they were doing, I swear. They weren't human at that moment. They were just crazy. These people went crazy. They took out all their frustrations on those two men. They deserved it. But I couldn't watch. And that's why I hate [INAUDIBLE]. Still, I, I love mankind. And I, I just pray for peace, that's all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There's a lot more to say, but I guess it will die with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you have something else you want to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of it right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You think you're finished now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's call it quits.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think we're done. Thanks. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can stop now. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ukraine ?] must have learned a lot. This is a picture of my mom and dad, Reva Potash, no Reva Tepper. And my father's name, Eli Potash at the time. They were engaged. And I think the year was 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the city and [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the city was Trochenbrod in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you  a question. As we look at the rest of these photographs, how do you happen to have these in your possession, since there was no way you could hold onto anything during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to the United States, I was very interested in any pictures that I saw my aunts and uncles have, and cousins. And I just took it from them. I said, I got-- I've got to have them. I was the only one, I guess, who was interested in it. And I accumulated. And I, and I took from everybody I could find pictures. And these were pictures originally mailed to them from Trochenbrod, from our shtetl. And when we came there-- came here, I either made copies or took the originals.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, next picture. As long as we get that story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quiet, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my favorite picture of all. This is a picture of my beloved shtetl, Trochenbrod. And my father is holding me on the extreme right. And I must be there just a little bit over a year old. So I would say this is 1931 or '32 in Trochenbrod, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love this picture, because my grandparents are in it-- on my father's side. And this picture was taken in 1932, I think, in Trochenbrod-- Zofiowka-- in Poland. And there was a time when my aunt-- my father's sister-- the fourth-- actually, in the center, in the top row center, came from the United States with her daughter, who is standing next to her, to visit us in Trochenbrod. And it was really, really a happy time for us all.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know there are a lot of people in this photograph. First of all, can you tell me where your parents are in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my parents are-- from the left is my uncle, and then is my father, and my, my mother is standing sideways.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are and your brothers in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am, from left, I am second from left with a ribbon in the hair. And next to me is my older brother and then my younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this in the bottom row you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bottom row, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now in this photograph, all the people-- of all the people in this picture, how many do you know actually survived the war?

words.text: of all the people in this

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five people survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's my parents, my brothers, and a cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Five.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very happy picture. This picture was taken on a Saturday afternoon. And it was customary to go after Shabbos dinner and nap to the park. And everyone here is in the park. And I can't imagine who would take this picture on a Shabbos afternoon, since everyone in the shtetl was orthodox. But there must have been one, one guy who didn't care. Or the audience didn't care. Anyway, and this, this picture was taken in the beginning of 1939. And my mother's in the center. To her-- on my, the right of her is my cousin, who was in the woods with us, and another cousin. And my brother is second from the left without hair. And the other people were people who were hiding with us in the woods. And one, two, three-- three people survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother and the two cousins-- husband and wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a miracle picture, because the little girl on the right with the bangs was the little girl that was in the woods who died. We covered her with leaves and came back to bury her at night. And she was still alive. And we nursed her back to health. Both children are cousins. They both survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken right after the war, in 1945, I think. And it was taken in Bindermichel-Linz in the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone needs to be quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in the DP camps in Linz, Bindermichel-Linz, Austria. And in the beginning-- it's 1946. And that was my father, my mother, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me their names again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the name is Reva and Eli Potash and Betty Potash, me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, everyone hold still be quiet, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wonderful picture. It's a happy [NON-ENGLISH] picture I call it. Because it was taken in Bindermichel-Linz, Austria, in 1946 in the DP camps. This was the first wedding of two refugees. And the gentleman in the hat is the groom. And the bride you can see with her hair piece-- or the veil. And on the left of them, to the far left, is my first boyfriend. His name is [? Chonia ?] [? Garfinkel ?], and he lives in Florida. And standing behind the bride and groom is my brother, Bernard, and I. And sitting next to them are their best friends, the couple's best friends. And then there's my mom, my dad, and, and a little friend from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me your parents' names again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reva and Eli Potash.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
